title: Why More Steps Can Mean Better Quality
concept: More denoising steps generally allow finer, more careful refinement, though at the cost of slower generation — a tradeoff explored later. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Diffusion vs. GANs vs. VAEs.'

exercises.prompt: When you're painting a picture, does taking more small careful strokes usually make the final art look more detailed?

options.text: Yes, more careful strokes usually add more detail
No, one big stroke is always better
Strokes don't affect detail at all

exercises.explain: More small, careful strokes let you refine details bit by bit — just like more denoising steps in diffusion models!

cards.title: One Big Leap or Many Small Steps?
cards.body: Imagine crossing a river. You could try one giant leap and risk falling in — or take many small careful steps across stones to reach the other side safely. Diffusion models face a similar choice when turning noise into an image!

cards.title: What Are Denoising Steps?
cards.body: Denoising steps are the number of small 'clean-up' moves a diffusion model makes to turn random noise into a clear image. More steps mean more chances to fix tiny mistakes and add fine detail — but each step takes time, so more steps also means slower generation.

cards.title: Like Sculpting Clay
cards.body: A sculptor who chips away at clay slowly, checking their work again and again, can carve fine details like eyelashes or wrinkles. A sculptor who rushes with just a few big chops gets a rough shape fast, but misses the fine details. More steps = more chances to refine!

cards.title: Generating a Portrait
cards.body: A diffusion model asked to draw a face might use 10 steps and get a blurry, rough face fast. Using 100 steps, it slowly refines the same face — sharper eyes, clearer hair strands, smoother skin — because each step gets another chance to polish the details.

cards.title: Myth: Infinite Steps = Perfect Image
cards.body: It's tempting to think 'more steps always means better forever' — but that's not quite true! After a certain point, extra steps add tiny improvements while taking much longer. There's a tradeoff between quality and speed, which we'll explore more later.

cards.title: Few Steps vs. Many Steps
cards.body: Comparing generation with fewer vs. more denoising steps shows a real quality-speed tradeoff.

left.text: Fast but blurry, rough edges, missing fine details

right.text: Slower but sharp, detailed, carefully refined

exercises.prompt: Why do more denoising steps generally improve image quality?

options.text: They give the model more chances to carefully refine details
They make the model add more random noise
They skip parts of the image entirely
They have no effect on the final image

exercises.explain: Each denoising step is a small refinement — more steps mean more opportunities to smooth out mistakes and sharpen details.

exercises.prompt: More denoising steps always make image generation faster.
exercises.explain: False! More steps usually mean slower generation, since each step takes extra time — that's the tradeoff.

exercises.sentence: More denoising steps generally allow for finer, more careful ___ of the image.
exercises.explain: Extra steps give the model more opportunities for refinement — small careful improvements to detail and clarity.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'I'll always use the maximum number of steps possible, since more steps only ever help and never cost anything.' What's missing from this thinking?

options.text: They're ignoring that more steps take more time to generate
They're right, there's no downside to more steps
More steps actually make images worse, so they should use fewer

exercises.explain: More steps generally improve quality, but they also slow down generation — that tradeoff is important to consider, not just quality alone.

exercises.prompt: A diffusion model generates images using either 20 steps or 200 steps. What's the most likely tradeoff between these two choices?

options.text: 20 steps: faster but rougher image. 200 steps: slower but more refined image
20 steps: slower but refined. 200 steps: faster but rougher
Both produce identical results with no differences
More steps always ruins the image completely

exercises.explain: Right! With fewer steps, generation is quick but details are rougher. With more steps, the model takes its time to carefully refine the image, resulting in better quality — at the cost of speed. This quality-vs-speed tradeoff is something we'll dig into more soon. Next up: Diffusion vs. GANs vs. VAEs.
